title: Your first Go program 🐹
instruction: Every Go program has a `func main()` — that's where it starts running. Click **Run & check** to see it in action.
successMessage: Boom — you just ran a Go program. No setup, no config files, no drama.

title: Announce Your Library
instruction: Change the message to `"Welcome to my library!"` and add a second Println for the hours: `"Open daily from 9 AM to 8 PM"`.
hint: Change the first Println and add a second one below it.
expectedOutput: Open daily from 9 AM to 8 PM
successMessage: Your library has a name and hours. The computer reads your code top-to-bottom, like a recipe.

title: Print the Book Count
instruction: Use `:=` to create a variable named `books` with value `1200`, then print a sentence using that variable.

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO lines with actual code.

successMessage: A variable holds a value so you can reuse it. The box has a label and a number inside.

title: Meet the Librarian
instruction: Create `name := "Alice"` and `books := 5000`, then print each on its own line.

codeChecks.message: Print books on a separate line
Replace the TODO lines with actual code.

successMessage: Two variables, two lines. Strings get quotes, numbers don't. Go knows the difference.

title: Comments are for humans 🤯
instruction: Lines starting with `//` are comments — Go ignores them. Add a `//` comment above the Println, then change the message to print `"Tomorrow at 9 AM"`.
hint: Add a // comment on its own line, then change the message.

codeChecks.message: Add a // comment on its own line above the fmt.Println call.

successMessage: Comments are free text — Go ignores them, humans read them. They explain why, not what.
